coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots modified ZnIn2S4 microspheres for visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution promotion performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Beibei
Xiao, Wen
Hu, Jiawei
Liu, Jinhong
Xu, Hui
Zheng, Xueqing
Wang, Wuyou
Wu, Haibo
Xi, Xinguo
Dong, Pengyu
Ji, Hongbing
coredata.dc:description: To effectively separate electron-hole pairs produced by light, a heterojunction arrangement can be employed, thereby improving photocatalytic efficiency. In this study, a simple hydrothermal process is used to manufacture carbonized polymer dots/ZnIn2S4 (CPDs/ZIS) heterostructure, which enhances the light absorption and charge carrier lifetime in comparison to bare ZnIn2S4 (ZIS). Upon irradiation with visible light, the 3-CPDs/ZIS composite generates hydrogen at a rate of 133 μmol g−1 h−1, which is 8.9 times faster than that of pure ZIS. The addition of CPDs can increase the range of light that can be absorbed, extend the service life of the optical charge, increase the specific surface area, and promote charge separation and transmission, which could effectively accelerate the photocatalytic reduction reaction. The presence of CPDs results in the introduction of multiple transition energy states and a decrease in the H* adsorption free energy, which enhances the hydrogen evolution activity according to the theoretical calculation findings of density functional theory (DFT) and Gibbs free energy of the hydrogen evolution process. Combining theoretical calculations and experimental results, a direct Z-type heterojunction mechanism is proposed for the hydrogen evolution promotion effectiveness of CPDs/ZIS under visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnIn2S4
CPDs
Z-scheme heterojunction
DFT calculation
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21403184 and 51772258), the Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province, the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (Grant no. 22KJA430008), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Yancheng Institute of Technology (KYCX22_XZ001). Furthermore, thanks for the assistance of Yancheng Institute of Technology's Analysis & Testing Center.

This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21403184 and 51772258 ), the Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province , the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (Grant no. 22KJA430008 ), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Yancheng Institute of Technology ( KYCX22_XZ001 ). Furthermore, thanks for the assistance of Yancheng Institute of Technology's Analysis & Testing Center.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM, (b) TEM, and (c) HRTEM images of 3-CPDs/ZIS. (d) STEM-HAADF image and EDS mapping images of 3-CPDs/ZIS corresponding to C, N, S, Zn, and In.

(a) XRD patterns and (b) UV–vis DRS spectra of ZIS, CPDs, and x-CPDs/ZIS composites (x = 1, 3, and 5), (c) corresponding (αhv)2 vs. hv plots for ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS, and (d) (αhv)1/2 vs. hv plot over CPDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the (a) survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s (d) Zn 2p, (e) In 3d, and (f) S 2p of ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS.
(a) FT-IR and (b) Raman spectra of ZIS, CPDs, and x-CPDs/ZIS (x = 1, 3, and 5) composites. (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (d) pore size distribution of ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS.

(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution and (b) H2 evolution rates of ZIS and x-CPDs/ZIS composites (x = 1, 3, and 5). (c) Four cycling tests for 3-CPDs/ZIS and (d) XRD patterns of 3-CPDs/ZIS before and after PHE cycling experiments. (The error bar was calculated using the standard error of three parallel tests.)

Atomistic modeling from side views of (a) ZIS and (b) CPDs/ZIS (110). Optimized H * (white) side view of the Zn position of the (c) ZIS(110) plane and (d) CPDs/ZIS (110) color scheme: purple: Zn; brown: In; yellow: S; gray: C; blue: N; white: H). Calculated DOS for the unit cell of (e) ZIS, (f) the ZIS(110) plane, and (g) CPDs/ZIS(110) with the width of bandgaps. (h) Gibbs free energy profiles for hydrogen evolution on ZIS (in black) and CPDs/ZIS (in red) with side views of optimized H* (in pink) on the Zn site.
(a) Transient photocurrent responses, (b) EIS spectra, (c) PL emission spectra, and (d) time-resolved PL (TR-PL) spectra.

Mott–Schottky plots of (a) CPDs and (b) ZIS. (c) Spin-trapping ESR spectra of •O2 − over pure ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS photocatalysts in the dark and after 10 min of visible light irradiation, and (d) the schematic Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism of 3-CPDs/ZIS.

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots modified ZnIn2S4 microspheres for visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution promotion performance

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Advanced Technology in Environmental Protection of Jiangsu Province, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To effectively separate electron-hole pairs produced by light, a heterojunction arrangement can be employed, thereby improving photocatalytic efficiency. In this study, a simple hydrothermal process is used to manufacture carbonized polymer dots/ZnIn2S4 (CPDs/ZIS) heterostructure, which enhances the light absorption and charge carrier lifetime in comparison to bare ZnIn2S4 (ZIS). Upon irradiation with visible light, the 3-CPDs/ZIS composite generates hydrogen at a rate of 133 μmol g−1 h−1, which is 8.9 times faster than that of pure ZIS. The addition of CPDs can increase the range of light that can be absorbed, extend the service life of the optical charge, increase the specific surface area, and promote charge separation and transmission, which could effectively accelerate the photocatalytic reduction reaction. The presence of CPDs results in the introduction of multiple transition energy states and a decrease in the H* adsorption free energy, which enhances the hydrogen evolution activity according to the theoretical calculation findings of density functional theory (DFT) and Gibbs free energy of the hydrogen evolution process. Combining theoretical calculations and experimental results, a direct Z-type heterojunction mechanism is proposed for the hydrogen evolution promotion effectiveness of CPDs/ZIS under visible light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, human beings have faced an increasing energy crisis due to excessive fossilfuelconsumption worldwide [1]. Consequently, it is critical to establish sustainable technology for creating “green” hydrogen (H2) energy. Photocatalytic water splitting into H2 production can transform solar energy into chemical fuel, which is seen as a sustainable and environmentally friendly strategy to potentially address the present energy crisis [2–4]. In recent decades, diverse semiconductor photocatalysts, that is, metal oxides [5,6], metal sulfides [7,8], graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [9], Ni-based compounds [10], and covalent organic frameworks [11–13], have been extensively studied in the H2 evolution process. Nevertheless, the majority of photocatalysts still exhibit low efficiency for H2 evolution due to restricted solar energy use and high charge carrier recombination rates. Thus, designing high-efficiency photocatalysts to address the aforementioned issues by boosting the charge carrier separation rate and light collecting capacity is a difficult task.

As typical photocatalysts, ternary metal sulfides generally possess narrow bandgap values and have been used in a variety of photocatalytic areas, such as H2 evolution, pollution degradation, Cr(VI) removal, and CO2 reduction. Among them, ZnIn2S4 is deemed a possible active photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution under visible light as a result of its controllable morphology, appropriate band gap for visible light absorption (2.06–2.85 eV), good chemical stability, and wide light response region [14–16]. However, for ZIS, fast charge carrier recombination efficiency and a limited ability to absorb visible light are two significant drawbacks that severely hinder the practical use of this material in photocatalytic domains [17]. Aiming to overcome these two shortcomings of ZIS, the photocatalytic performance has been significantly enhanced by designing heterostructured ZIS-based photocatalysts, which can offer an intriguing alternative for the fulfillment of the requirements for charge carrier separation and light harvesting [18–21]. Particularly, constructing Z-scheme ZIS-based heterojunctions can preserve the strong redox capability of photogenerated charge carriers while also encouraging the spatial separation of those charge carriers [22].

As a zero-dimensional material, carbon polymer dots (CPDs) have hybridized structures of polymer and graphitized carbon, showing abundant functional groups and polymer chains on their surface [23]. Due to their remarkable characteristics, such as low price, safety, chemical stability, broad range of light absorption, and effective electron transfer capacity, CPDs are frequently used in a range of applications [24]. Notably, CPDs have the potential to be superior electron acceptors and transporters because of their special conjugated π structure. Incorporating CPDs with other semiconductor photocatalysts can effectively facilitate charge separation, broaden the light harvesting region and thereby enhance the photocatalytic efficiency.

Building a Z-scheme CPDs/ZIS composite is projected to attain a high photocatalytic efficiency based on the characteristics of ZIS and CPDs. Based on the above considerations, one-step hydrothermal fabrication of the Z-scheme CPDs/ZIS composites with microspherical morphology was accomplished. A series of CPDs/ZIS composites were thoroughly studied to determine their morphology, crystal structure, atomic states, and light absorption capability. Furthermore, the as-prepared samples' photocatalytic H2 evolution (PHE) performance was evaluated. This work pays much attention to the following issues: (1) the performance of the Z-scheme CPDs/ZIS heterojunction for PHE; (2) the roles of introduced CPDs in promoting the capability for light gathering and aiding charge carrier separation; (3) the determined factors for enhancing PHE by experimental results along with theoretical calculation; and (4) the mechanism of CPDs/ZIS for PHE.

Zinc chloride (ZnCl2, AR, 99.0 %), indium chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O, AR, 99.99 %), thioacetamide (TAA, AR, 98.0 %), sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3, AR, 98.0 %) and sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, AR, 98.0 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, AR, 99.5 %), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, AR, 99.0 %), sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4, AR, 99.0 %) and ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, AR, 99.5 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents utilized in this work were analytical grade and were not been purified further.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were first prepared by a straightforward one-step solvothermal technique [25]. Typically, 10 mL of deionized water was used to dissolve 5 mmol of citric acid monohydrate and 335 μL of ethylenediamine separately. After that, the mixture was heated at 200 °C for 5 h in a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The obtained suspension was dialyzed for 24 h, cooled naturally to room temperature for 48 h, and then freeze-dried. Finally, the black product of CPDs was achieved.

To prepare CPDs/ZnIn2S4 (CPDs/ZIS) composites, ZnCl2 (136 mg), InCl3·4H2O (586 mg), and TAA (301 mg) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (30 mL). After that, a certain number of CPDs were added. The combined solution was placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C after vigorous stirring for 1 h. The reactors were heated for 6 h before cooling naturally to room temperature. The samples were filtered before being vacuum dried at 60 °C overnight and repeatedly washed with ethanol and deionized water. The resulting products were denoted as x-CPDs/ZIS (x = 1, 3, and 5), where x wt% represents the theoretical mass ratio of CPDs to ZIS. For comparison, the identical procedure used to create CPDs/ZIS composites without CPDs was used to create bulk ZIS.

To examine the microstructure of the synthesized materials, JEOL JEM-200CX and JEOL JEM-6300F were employed for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM), respectively. Using a JEOL JEM-200CX microscope, images in scanning transmission mode (STEM) were also obtained. On a Bruker D8 diffractometer, X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Philip) were measured using Cu Kα radiation. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Escalab 250 Xi) with Mg Kα radiation was used. A UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600plus) was used to determine the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS). The traditional KBr disk method was used to evaluate the Fourier transform spectrophotometer (FT-IR, Nexus 470, Thermo Electron Corporation). A Raman spectroscope (DXR, Thermo Fisher) was used to obtain Raman scattering spectra. Using a specific surface area analyzer (Beckman Coulter SA3100), the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and pore structure of the materials were assessed. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were determined using a 48000DSCF luminescence spectrometer. An F900 fluorescence spectrometer was used to confirm the time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) decay spectra of the photocatalysts with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide (DMPO) electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrum was utilized to determine the •O2 − generated by the photocatalytic reaction system.

Along with a traditional three-electrode cell, an electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, CHI-660D) was used to evaluate the photoelectrochemical characteristics of the samples. The counter and reference electrodes were made of platinum (Pt) wire and Ag/AgCl, respectively. The fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass-based working electrode was fabricated as follows: 10.0 mg photocatalyst was added into the mixed solution (50 μL) containing distilled water (20 μL), ethanol (20 μL) and Nafion (10 μL) under ultrasonic treatment. Subsequently, the formed homogeneous suspension was spin-coated onto the FTO glass (1 × 1 cm2) surface. After natural drying, the resulting FTO working electrode was heated at 120 °C for 5 h. The photocurrents and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra of the as-prepared samples were measured in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution, and a 300 W Xenon arc lamp (λ > 420 nm) was used as a light source.

The photocatalytic performance of the samples was investigated by the hydrogen evolution process under visible light using a quartz-side irradiation reactor. A light source of 300 W Xe light with a filter (λ > 420 nm) was employed for PHE experiments. In a typical procedure, 220 mL of distilled water was introduced to a reactor along with 100 mg of photocatalyst, Na2S·9H2O (0.35 M), and Na2SO3 (0.25 M). The photocatalytic device was repeatedly vacuumed and filled with argon (Ar) gas to entirely remove air before the PHE reaction. After stirring for 5 min and subsequent sonication for 30 s, the reactor was connected to the photocatalytic evaluation system for PHE experiments under visible light. The amount of H2 generated was measured using gas chromatography (Shimadzu, GC-2014) outfitted with a thermal conductive detector (TCD). The photocatalytic cycling experiments of 3-CPDs/ZIS were carried out for four runs to assess the photocatalytic stability.

The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) in the QUANTUM-ESPRESSO (QE) package of the Heyd-Scuseria-Ernzerhof screened hybrid functional (HSE06) to the exchange–correlation function was performed to calculate the electronic structure and bandgap value of the as-prepared samples using gamma Monkhorst-Pack k-point sampling [26–28]. The actual space grid's cutoff energy was set at 250 Ry, while the kinetic cutoff energy for a plane-wave function was set at 35 Ry. The optimized lattice parameter of ZIS was 10.7139 Å. For the ZIS unit cell, the bandgap value was calculated to be 2.10 eV with 2 × 2 × 2 Monkhorst-Pack k-point sampling. In addition, the ZIS (110) plane was modeled by a p (1 × 1) supercell with five atomic layers. A mixed structure of carbon and nitrogen atoms was deposited on the ZIS (110) plane to model the CPDs. A 2 × 2 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-point sampling was used for calculating the electric structure of the ZIS (110) plane. The two layers on the bottom were fixed, and other atoms were relaxed during geometry optimizations.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and enlarged high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the 3-CPDs/ZIS are shown in Fig. 1 . The flower-like morphology of 3-CPDs/ZIS is clearly visible with a hierarchical structure that is composed of ultrathin nanosheets (Fig. 1a). In contrast to ZIS (Fig. S1), many dark spots are observed on the surface of the nanosheets, which indicates that CPDs are formed and closely combined with ZIS (Fig. 1b). In the HRTEM image of 3-CPDs/ZIS (Fig. 1c), the lattice fringe spacing of 0.193 nm belongs to the (110) crystal plane of the ZIS. The high-angle annular dark field (STEM-HAADF) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping images (Fig. 1d) show a homogeneous distribution of C/N/S/Zn/In elements on the 3-CPDs/ZIS, further indicating the successful synthesis of the 3-CPDs/ZIS.

The phase structures of ZIS, CPDs, and x-CPDs/ZIS (x = 1, 3, and 5) composites were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. As seen in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks of the as-synthesized ZIS are compatible with the reported hexagonal ZIS (JCPDS: No. 65-2023) [29]. A broad peak at approximately 25° appeared in the XRD pattern of the CPDs, confirming their successful preparation. With the addition of CPDs, the diffraction peaks of x-CPDs/ZIS samples are similar to those of pure ZIS, demonstrating that the addition of CPDs has no noticeable impact on the ZIS crystal structure [30]. Notably, there are no evident characteristic peaks of CPDs in all CPDs/ZIS samples, which is probably due to the fact that CPDs' low loading content is below the XRD apparatus's detectable limits [31,32].

The optical absorption property and bandgap energy of different samples were studied by UV–vis absorption spectra. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the light absorption edge of pristine ZIS appears at 565 nm, showing that it has a high potential for light harvesting in both the ultraviolet and visible light ranges. The UV–vis DRS spectrum of CPDs has two absorption peaks at 331 and 478 nm, showing a strong and broad UV–vis absorption owing to its π–π* and n–π* transitions [32]. With increasing CPDs content, all x-CPDs/ZIS (x = 1, 3, and 5) composites show a broadened light absorption region and gradual redshifted absorption edge related to pure ZIS.

In addition, the bandgap energy (E g) can be obtained based on the following equation: αhv = A (hv − E g) n
/2, where α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and optical frequency, respectively [33,34]. The n value is dependent on the direct (n = 1) or indirect transition (n = 4) of semiconductors. Considering the direct semiconductor for ZIS and indirect semiconductor for CPDs [35,36], the bandgap energies of ZIS and CPDs are calculated to be 2.32 and 0.69 eV, respectively (Fig. 2c and 2d). As the loading amount of CPDs in 3-CPDs/ZIS is only 3 wt%, the bandgap value of 3-CPDs/ZIS is calculated as 2.02 eV via a plot of (αhv)2 versus hv (Fig. 2c). As a result, it is plausible to conclude that CPDs loading can considerably improve the light-harvesting capability and electric structure of ZIS.

The surface chemical composition and chemical states of ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The 3-CPDs/ZIS sample comprises Zn, In, S, C, and N elements in the XPS survey spectrum, suggesting the coexistence of CPDs and ZIS in 3-CPDs/ZIS (Fig. 3 a). The C 1s XPS spectrum of 3-CPDs/ZIS is fitted into three peaks at 284.5, 285.9, and 288.2 eV, which are assigned to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO, respectively (Fig. 3b) [37]. The N 1s XPS spectra of 3-CPDs/ZIS were fitted to two peaks at 400.4 and 399.3 eV, which were assigned to NH and CN, respectively (Fig. 3c) [25]. The combined results of XPS C 1s and N 1s reveal the generation of CPDs in 3-CPDs/ZIS.

Two peaks, Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of Zn2+, which are present at energies of 1022.0 and 1045.1 eV, respectively[38], are visible in the Zn 2p XPS spectrum of pure ZIS (Fig. 3d). The characteristic peaks at 444.9 and 452.5 eV over ZIS are assigned to In 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of In3+ (Fig. 3e), while the S 2p peaks of ZIS centered at 162.9 and 161.7 eV are attributed to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 of S2−, respectively (Fig. 3f) [39,40]. Notably, the XPS Zn 2p, In 3d, and S 2p peaks of 3-CPDs/ZIS are shifted to a lower binding energy than those of single ZIS (Fig. 3d–f), demonstrating the existence of a significant interaction between CPDs and ZIS [41,42].

Fourier transform spectrophotometer (FT-IR) spectra were measured to confirm the existence of CPDs in the CPDs/ZIS composites. Compared with ZIS, all CPDs/ZIS composites show three characteristic peaks at 1710, 1646, and 1540 cm−1, corresponding to CO, CN, and NH of CPDs, respectively (Fig. 4 a) [25], which indicates the presence of CPDs in CPDs/ZIS composites. Notably, all CPDs/ZIS composites possess a relatively weak peak intensity compared to pure CPDs due to the small loading amount of CPDs in CPDs/ZIS composites. In addition, the prepared pure ZIS and CPDs/ZIS composites were further demonstrated by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4b, pure CPDs and CPDs/ZIS composites all exhibit two Raman signals at approximately 1393 and 1594 cm−1, which are assigned to the defected (D) carbon and crystalline graphite (G) bands, respectively [24]. Thus, the results of FT-IR and Raman spectra further imply that CPDs have been successfully combined with ZIS to form heterostructured photocatalysts, which is beneficial to charge carrier separation and transfer [43].

To examine the Brunauer − Emmett − Teller (BET) surface area and pore distribution of pure ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were developed. As displayed in Fig. 4c, both pure ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS exhibit type IV adsorption isotherms, and their hysteresis loop characteristics with a relative pressure of P/P 0 = 0.45 ∼ 1 are ascribed to the H3 type [44]. This demonstrates that both pure ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS display a mesoporous structure derived from nanosheet-containing spheres. The BET surface areas, pore volumes, and average pore sizes of the ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS samples were determined and are displayed in Table S1. The BET surface area of 3-CPDs/ZIS (33.0 m2/g) is higher than that of pure ZIS (25.6 m2/g), owing to the increased surface roughness by decorating CPDs onto the ZIS surface. The pore size distribution curves are calculated using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) technique (Fig. 4d), which implies that the porosity of 3-CPDs/ZIS rises dramatically with the addition of CPDs to ZIS. Consequently, the large surface area and porous structure of 3-CPDs/ZIS can offer more reactive sites for the PHE reaction.

Using Na2SO3/Na2S as sacrificial agents, the photocatalytic H2 evolution (PHE) activity of the samples was assessed under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). As shown in Fig. 5 a, pure ZIS exhibits the lowest PHE activity, which is attributed to the high recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes [45]. After loading CPDs, the x-CPDs/ZIS composites (x = 1, 3, and 5) show enhanced PHE activities related to pure ZIS. The increased photocatalytic activity is attributed to the synergistic effects of the increased charge carrier separation rate and expanded light gathering capacity, which stems from CPDs deposition. In particular, the optimal sample of 3-CPDs/ZIS, with CPDs loading amount of 3 wt%, possesses a higher PHE efficiency (133 μmol g−1 h−1), which is 8.9 times more than that of pure ZIS (Fig. 5b). Notably, the excess loading amount of CPDs in 5-CPDs/ZIS can result in a decreased PHE efficiency. Because the excessively deposited CPDs in 5-CPDs/ZIS will decrease the overall light absorption capacity and consequently cause a relative decline in the total amount of photogenerated charge carriers, the PHE activity will be reduced. As demonstrated in Table S2, the H2 evolution rate of 3-CPDs/ZIS without any cocatalyst outperformed that of certain previously reported photocatalysts based on CPDs and ZIS. In addition, the structural stability and reusability of 3-CPDs/ZIS were tested by PHE experiments for four cycling runs. After four runs, the PHE activity is not significantly reduced, indicating the excellent photocatalytic stability of 3-CPDs/ZIS (Fig. 5c). The XRD patterns and XPS survey spectra of the 3-CPDs/ZIS samples are almost unchanged before and after PHE experiments for four runs (Fig. 5d, S2), manifesting the high structural stability of 3-CPDs/ZIS.

Density functional theory (DFT) was used to calculate the electrical structures of the CPDs/ZIS heterojunction using the hybrid HSE06 function. After geometric optimization, the side views of the ZIS and (b) CPDs/ZIS (110) are presented in Fig. 6 a, b. The calculated density of state (DOS) of the ZIS unit cell is shown in Fig. 6e. As the bandgap value is equal to the difference between the VBM and CBM of the semiconductor, the band gap of the ZIS unit cell (Exp. ∼ 2.40 eV) is estimated to be ∼2.10 eV. This indicates the high reliability of the hybrid HSE06 function for investing in electronic structures.

As depicted in Fig. 6f, g, the bandgap values of the ZIS (110) plane and CPDs/ZIS (110) are determined to be 1.57 and 1.41 eV, respectively. The deposition of CPDs onto the ZIS surface can introduce some transition energy states in the middle gap of the ZIS. These transition states across the Fermi level can profoundly improve the light absorption efficiency. Moreover, these occupied states in the middle of the gap play two dominant roles in reducing the bandgap value from 1.57 to 1.41 eV and facilitating electron migration via the polaron hopping mechanism.

The optimized H* (white) side view on the Zn position of the ZIS (110) plane and CPDs/ZIS (110) are shown in Fig. 6c, d. In addition, theoretical calculations indicate that the adsorption free energy of H* is assigned to the 1/2 H2 gas on the Zn site at the ZIS (110) plane (Fig. 6h). It can be clearly observed that the H* value of 0.87 eV for ZIS is reduced to 0.63 eV for CPDs/ZIS (110), demonstrating that the addition of CPDs on the surface contributes to the reduction of the H* adsorption free energy over ZIS on the original (110) plane. The H* adsorption free energy for ideal catalysts should be close to zero according to Sabatier's hydrogen evolution principles. Thus, it is reasonable to infer that the enhanced hydrogen evolution activity of ZIS is achieved by tuning the H* binding energy in the presence of CPDs.

Photoelectrochemical technology can be utilized to examine the interfacial charge separation efficiency [46]. Fig. 7 a shows the photocurrent responses of pure ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS's numerous switching cycles while exposed to visible light. The photocurrent intensity of 3-CPDs/ZIS (0.25 μA cm−2) is approximately 1.5 times that of a single ZIS, demonstrating that the loading of CPDs can facilitate the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. By using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the interfacial charge transfer resistance of the as-prepared samples was identified. Typically, the carrier transfer resistance decreases as the arc size increases [47]. As displayed in Fig. 7b, the Nyquist semicircle of the 3-CPDs/ZIS sample possesses a smaller diameter than that of pure ZIS. This demonstrates that the introduction of CPDs onto the ZIS surface can improve the charge separation efficiency and facilitate interfacial charge transfer. Consequently, it is possible to conclude that the construction of a heterojunction between CPDs and ZIS benefits the efficient separation and transfer of photoinduced electrons and holes, thereby boosting PHE activity.

The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved transient photoluminescence (TR-PL) results were obtained to further confirm the above findings. The PL intensity of CPDs-modified ZIS composites under photoexcitation (310 nm) is significantly lower than that of pure ZIS, as shown in Fig. 7c, indicating that CPDs can effectively transfer electrons and suppress photogenerated carrier reorganization. TR-PL spectroscopy and fitting of the samples' emission decay curves with a double exponential kinetic function were used to further elucidate the mean lifetime (τ) of photoinduced charges in the ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS composites (Fig. 7d). The average lifetime (2.42 ns) of the 3-CPDs/ZIS composite is longer than that of pristine ZIS (1.47 ns). The PL carrier radiative lifetime of CPDs-modified ZIS composites is significantly increased, which is caused by the effective separation of photoinduced electrons and holes and the efficient photoelectron transfer from the conduction band of ZIS to CPDs. The efficient photoactivity of the optimized composites was fully supported by steady-state PL studies and TR-PL decay spectroscopy. The linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) spectra of the monomeric ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS composites are shown in Fig. S3. The overpotential of 3-CPDs/ZIS is less than that of ZIS, implying that the inclusion of CPDs in the composite may speed up the separation of light-induced electron-hole pairs and favor H2 generation.

The Mott–Schottky (M–S) profiles of the ZIS and CPDs were gathered at AC frequencies of 1.0 and 1.5 kHz, respectively, to clarify the potential photocatalytic mechanism of the CPDs/ZIS heterojunction catalysts. As shown in Fig. 8 a and 8b, all Mott–Schottky curves have positive slopes, proving that each sample is an n-type semiconductor. For n-type semiconductors, the flat-charged field is more positive 0.1 V than the conduction band field [27]. The flat-charged positions of the ZIS and CPDs are −1.09 and −1.31 eV (vs. AgCl/Ag at pH = 7), respectively. Thus, the calculated ECB for the ZIS and CPDs were −1.19 and −1.41 eV, respectively. Based on the formula ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 [28], the flat charged position can be converted to −0.99 and −1.21 eV (vs. NHE, normal hydrogen electrode). Furthermore, according to the band-gap results obtained by the DRS spectrum, the valence band (VB) potentials of the original ZIS and CPDs are 1.33 and −0.52 eV, respectively.

To obtain more information on the photogenerated charge migration mechanism of CPDs/ZIS heterojunctions, •O2 − radicals in ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS were investigated using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide (DMPO) electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 8c, no ESR signal was detected in the dark. After 10 min of light irradiation, the 3-CPDs/ZIS and ZIS presented the DMPO-•O2 − signal simultaneously. The ESR spectra of the DMPO-•O2 − of 3-CPDs/ZIS were substantially higher than those of pure ZIS (Fig. 8c), showing that the addition of CPDs helps to provide more electrons in the process of lowering O2 to form •O2 −. The ZIS and CPDs have conduction band (CB) values of −0.99 eV and −1.21 eV, respectively, which are both lower than the potential (−0.33 eV versus NHE) of Eθ (O2/•O2 −), with the more negative CB position of CPDs indicating that CPDs are more likely to create •O2 − than ZIS when exposed to visible light. In conjunction with the above study, a putative mechanism of photocatalytic hydrogen generation by CPDs/ZIS heterojunctions was postulated. The CPDs/ZIS are inferred to be either type II or type Z heterostructures based on the band structure of the heterostructure. When light is excited in type II heterostructures, the h+ on the VB of ZIS will move to the VB of CPDs, and the CB of CPDs will move to the CB of ZIS. However, the theoretical difference between the VB of CPDs and the CB of ZIS is quite minimal, which makes it simple for electrons in the CB of ZIS to reach the VB of CPDs, speeding electron-hole rearrangement and considerably limiting carrier migration and separation. Therefore, the likelihood of CPDs/ZIS being a direct Z-type heterojunction structure is higher (Fig. 8d). When incident light has higher energy than photons, all materials produce light-generating electrons and holes when excited by visible light. Electrons on the CB of ZIS rebind to holes on the VB of CPDs, and electrons in the ZIS VB and CPDs CB still maintain strong redox capacity. The electrons on the CB of CPDs can interact with proton to produce more H2. Photogenerated holes in ZIS VB will be captured by a sacrificial hole scavenger adsorbed on the material surface, thus further enhancing the photocatalyzed H2 generation reaction.

In summary, a novel Z-type CPDs/ZIS heterostructured photocatalyst was successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal technique for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. When the optimal CPDs loading was 3 wt%, the prepared CPDs/ZIS composite showed the highest PHE photocatalytic performance (133 μmol g−1 h−1), which was 8.9 times higher than that of pure ZIS. The superior photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of this heterostructure can be attributed to the following main factors: the anchoring of CPDs onto the surface of ZIS results in the accumulation of electrons in the CB of CPDs, leading to the generation of more superoxide radicals. The ESR analysis of the photocatalytic system and the determination of the semiconductor energy level indicate that the CPDs/ZIS catalyst operates via a Z-scheme photocatalytic system. The Z-scheme mechanism in the heterojunction facilitates the separation of photogenerated carriers while retaining their strong redox ability, thereby enhancing the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction. The theoretical calculation results show that the surface-improved CPDs introduce some transition energy states in the middle of the ZIS gap, and these transition states across the Fermi level can significantly improve the adsorption efficiency of light. Meanwhile, the modification of CPDs on the surface also leads to increased hydrogen evolution activity by a decrease in the free energy of H* adsorption. Overall, this work mainly emphasizes the role of CPDs materials in improving the performance of ZIS semiconductors for photocatalytic hydrogen production in comparison to previously reported based on CPDs photocatalysts [35,37,43,48,49], which provides an important reference to design more efficient CPDs-based photocatalysts by making full use of the advantageous properties of CPDs.

Beibei Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Wen Xiao: . Jiawei Hu: Software. Jinhong Liu: Validation. Hui Xu: Data curation. Xueqing Zheng: Data curation. Wuyou Wang: . Haibo Wu: . Xinguo Xi: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Pengyu Dong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hongbing Ji: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21403184 and 51772258), the Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province, the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (Grant no. 22KJA430008), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Yancheng Institute of Technology (KYCX22_XZ001). Furthermore, thanks for the assistance of Yancheng Institute of Technology's Analysis & Testing Center.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.08.057.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
Addition Figures and Tables: SEM and TEM images of ZIS; XPS spectra of 3-CPDs/ZIS before and after PHE cycling experiments; LSV curves of ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS; the Table of BET surface areas, pore volumes, average pore sizes of ZIS and 3-CPDs/ZIS and summary of H2 evolution activity of some CPDs-based and ZIS-based photocatalytic systems.
